The Catalog

Four entries — a museum-commissioned systems project, a solo VR build, a UX research piece, and an earlier project on intercultural competence and accessible design.

I

Opbouwdag

Role
Programmer
Tools
Unity · C# · TextMeshPro
Context
Commissioned by the Victory Museum
Status
In development — final term

Opbouwdag follows a player returning to a town left in ruins at the end of the Second World War and rebuilding it one building at a time. Rather than fighting, the player restores — clearing rubble, refurbishing interiors, and restocking each building until the town is whole again, with each building's three stages handled by a short, distinct minigame. The game frames the war through its aftermath: no weapons, no enemies, just a hopeful arc of recovery, designed to stay approachable for players aged 8 to 13.

As one of the programmers on the team, I designed a core piece of the project's architecture: an abstract BaseMiniGame class that every minigame inherits from, sharing input handling, win conditions, and scene transitions while remaining fully independent. Around it I built the MiniGameManager, InputManager, GameManager, a scene-flow wrapper, a save bridge for persisting building progress, and a shared feedback system for audio and visual cues.

Mini-game architecture — InputManager and MiniGameManager run whichever BaseMiniGame is current; five concrete minigames implement it independently.

One constraint shaped every decision: never modify a teammate's existing code. Everything had to integrate through public methods only — which meant the architecture had to be legible enough for the rest of the team, most of them beginners, to extend on their own. That question became my research focus: how to structure a mini-game architecture for rapid implementation by beginner developers using an abstract class system in Unity.

I also built five of the minigames directly: a swipe-based sorting task and a memory-match game for the Library, and a spot-and-tap search, a sliding puzzle with a skip mechanism, and a sequence-ordering task for the Town Hall.

The town map, and the Library sorting minigame mid-restoration

II

The Hive

Role
Solo Developer
Tools
Unity · PC VR (Meta Quest)
Context
Coursework
Status
Ongoing

The Hive is a first-person VR survival-management experience set thirty years in the future, in a world where climate change is no longer a prediction but the baseline reality. The player is a state-assigned grower, cultivating plants in hexagonal balconies that serve as the last remaining source of fuel, oxygen, and power for the wealthy. Plants function as currency: the loop is receive seeds and government-issued growing pods, grow, harvest, package, and hand the result over to a government drone in exchange for compensation.

As solo developer, the core technical challenge is the VR interaction system underlying that loop — handling planting, growth state, harvesting, and packaging as trackable, currency-like resources within a hexagonal balcony layout, set against a solarpunk-meets-dystopia visual language of muted greys, yellow tech accents, and controlled greenery.

Comfort is also part of that system: the player can switch between continuous (smooth) turning and snap turning at any time, to prevent VR-induced nausea. Using the Meta XR Interaction Toolkit's left and right controller rig already present in the scene, a TurnManager script listens for changes on a world-space dropdown and toggles smoothTurnEnabled on each controller's input manager accordingly — switching between the SnapTurnProvider and ContinuousTurnProvider without leaving the headset.

III

Egg, Inc. — UI/UX Heuristic Redesign

Role
UX Researcher & Designer
Tools
Figma · Nielsen's Heuristics
Context
Coursework — Human-Computer Interaction
Status
Complete

A full heuristic evaluation of Egg, Inc., a mobile idle game, against Nielsen's ten usability heuristics combined with the broader UX pillars of usability, accessibility, and ethics.

The evaluation identified six heuristic violations across the home screen, challenges menu, and shipping depot — including poor visibility of system status, inconsistent layouts between screens, and unclear affordances around the depot interaction. Each issue moved through paper sketches, low-fidelity wireframes, and a final high-fidelity Figma prototype, with every redesigned element labelled against the heuristic it addresses.

The deliverable is a full report — methodology, findings, redesign proposals, and rationale — alongside an interactive prototype walking through the corrected flows.

Figma prototype

Egg, Inc. redesigned home screen (hi-fi), full Figma prototype overview, and hatch-button capacity states

IV

Tidy Spell

Role
Solo Developer
Tools
GameMaker
Context
Coursework — Intercultural Competence
Status
Ongoing

A cozy isometric puzzle game set in a whimsical magical academy where everyday objects are alive — books flap like birds, towels wiggle like pets, backpacks crawl around sleepily. The player is a first-year student living away from home for the first time, learning to share space with roommates from different cultural backgrounds. Each level is a confined space — a kitchen shelf, a bathroom cabinet, a dorm desk — where the player arranges their belongings, decides what to keep, and stays mindful of roommates' space and shared areas.

Intercultural competence is built into the objects themselves: each roommate's belongings reflect their background, traditions, or values, so players discover diversity through interaction rather than exposition. Accessibility shaped the core controls — mouse-only input with switchable left- and right-handed play — and the feedback loop: objects sparkle when placed well, slump when a space is overcrowded, or wiggle nervously when misplaced, with no penalty for getting it wrong.

My contribution covers two of the project's core accessibility systems. The dragging system lets the player pick up and reposition any item with a single mouse button — on click, the offset between the cursor and the item's origin point is stored, so the item moves naturally from wherever it was grabbed rather than snapping to the cursor's centre. The tooltip system toggles with the right mouse button, using position_meeting() inside a with() loop to detect whichever item the cursor is hovering, then fades a tooltip box in or out via linear interpolation to reveal that item's name, likes, avoids, and a hint. Both systems keep every interaction on a single input device, by design.

Tidy Spell — the tooltip accessibility feature revealing an item's placement preferences

Provenance

Where the systems-first approach comes from, and what else is in the workshop.

I'm a second-year Creative Media & Game Technologies student, focused on programming, with a long-running interest in history — particularly that of Bulgaria, my home country.

When I started programming, the thing that nearly stopped me wasn't the syntax — it was the mess. Information and files scattered everywhere, no clear place for anything. That early confusion is the reason almost everything I build now starts with the structure first: a system I can read at a glance, that someone else could pick up and extend without asking me how it works.

History feeds the same instinct from a different angle. I'm drawn to settings with a clear internal logic — how the Cyrillic alphabet was systematised at Bulgaria's Preslav Literary School to fit Slavic speech, how a town rebuilds itself after a war — and to game ideas that turn up however they turn up, including, more than once, a dream I woke up still thinking about.

Outside of Unity, I've been getting into Blender — modelling, sculpting, and eventually 3D printing — and I have a background in accounting, which probably explains why I like things that balance.
